h1Title: Histoplasmosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Histoplasmosis is a fungal lung infection caused by Histoplasma capsulatum, a dimorphic fungus that grows as a mold in soil enriched by bird or bat droppings and converts to a yeast form at body temperature after spore inhalation. An estimated 500,000 people in the United States are infected each year, with the highest incidence along the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys where 60-90% of long-term residents show serologic evidence of prior exposure. Most infections are asymptomatic or self-limited, presenting as a flu-like illness 3-17 days after heavy exposure. Immunocompromised patients face progressive disseminated disease with mortality exceeding 25% if untreated. Antifungal therapy with itraconazole or amphotericin B is highly effective when started promptly.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 500,000 new infections per year in the US (CDC); seroprevalence up to 90% in endemic Ohio and Mississippi river valley communities
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; severe disease disproportionately affects HIV-positive patients (CD4 <150 cells/µL), solid-organ transplant recipients, and infants
keyStats.avgAge: Symptomatic acute pulmonary disease most common in adults aged 20-50 years with high-exposure occupations; dissemination more likely at age extremes
keyStats.globalCases: Worldwide distribution in temperate and subtropical zones; Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia report significant burden alongside the United States

definition: Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis (ICD-10: B39.0) is the primary symptomatic lung presentation of Histoplasma capsulatum infection, a dimorphic fungal pathogen that resides in soil containing nitrogen-enriched organic matter, primarily from bird (starling, blackbird) and bat excreta. On inhalation, conidia (spores) are phagocytosed by alveolar macrophages, convert to budding yeasts at 37°C, and replicate intracellularly. In immunocompetent hosts, T-cell-mediated immunity contains infection within 2-4 weeks; residual pulmonary nodules and hilar calcifications serve as radiographic evidence of resolved disease. When the fungal inoculum is very high — as in microbial explosions during demolition, spelunking, or cleaning chicken coops — acute symptomatic illness breaks through even in normal immunity. Acute pulmonary disease spans a spectrum from mild self-limited pneumonitis to moderate illness with mediastinal lymphadenopathy, pericarditis, and arthritis-erythema nodosum complexes. In patients with underlying structural lung disease such as COPD or emphysema, cavitary chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis can develop, resembling tuberculosis. Infectious disease specialists manage confirmed and suspected cases; pulmonology, rheumatology, and immunology co-manage complex presentations.

typesClassification.description: Flu-like illness 3-17 days after exposure in immunocompetent individuals. Fever, headache, dry cough, myalgia, and chest discomfort resolve spontaneously over 2-4 weeks without antifungal therapy in most cases. Hilar adenopathy on chest imaging is characteristic.

Exposure to massive spore loads (demolition sites, bat caves) can produce acute respiratory distress syndrome-like illness even in immunocompetent hosts. Diffuse bilateral infiltrates, hypoxia, and respiratory failure require antifungal therapy and sometimes mechanical ventilation.

Occurs in immunocompromised patients, infants, and elderly. Yeast disseminates hematogenously to reticuloendothelial system, liver, spleen, adrenals, oropharyngeal mucosa, and CNS. Without treatment, mortality exceeds 25%.

Mediastinal lymphadenitis and mediastinal granuloma can compress adjacent structures (airways, esophagus, superior vena cava). Fibrosing mediastinitis — a rare late sequel — is irreversible and not improved by antifungal therapy.
Occurs in patients with pre-existing emphysema; resembles pulmonary tuberculosis with apical cavities, weight loss, and productive cough. Requires prolonged itraconazole therapy for 12-24 months.

primarySymptoms: Fever of 38-40°C with chills, onset 3-17 days after fungal spore inhalation — the most consistent symptom across all severity levels of acute disease.
Dry, non-productive cough that persists 1-3 weeks and may worsen before improving, sometimes accompanied by pleuritic chest pain from adjacent pleural inflammation.
Diffuse myalgia and marked fatigue that frequently prevents normal activity for 1-2 weeks and is disproportionate to the degree of fever.
Headache, often frontal or retro-orbital, present in roughly 50% of symptomatic cases and sometimes severe enough to prompt meningitis workup.
Hilar or mediastinal lymphadenopathy visible on chest radiograph in 40-70% of symptomatic cases — an important clue that distinguishes histoplasmosis from community-acquired bacterial pneumonia.
Joint pain (arthralgias or true arthritis) of wrists, ankles, or knees, often with an erythema nodosum-like tender red nodular rash on the shins, in 5-10% of symptomatic patients.
Dyspnea or mild exertional breathlessness in moderate disease, progressing to resting shortness of breath and hypoxia in heavy-inoculum or immunocompromised cases.
Pericarditis-type chest pain — sharp, positional, radiating to the trapezius ridge — in 5-6% of symptomatic histoplasmosis due to mediastinal lymph node involvement adjacent to the pericardium.
Weight loss of 2-5 kg over the acute illness, particularly in patients with heavy exposure or subclinical chronic disease not yet diagnosed.
Oral ulcers, hepatosplenomegaly, and pancytopenia in patients with progressive dissemination, signaling systemic reticuloendothelial involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: Flu-like illness with fever, myalgia, and dry cough appearing 1-2 weeks after a known high-risk exposure — demolition of old buildings, spelunking, cleaning chicken coops, or landscaping disturbing soil near bird roosts
Unexplained hilar lymphadenopathy on chest X-ray in a patient from or recently in the central or eastern United States, even without respiratory symptoms
Persistent fatigue and low-grade fever lasting more than 3 weeks in an immunocompromised patient from an endemic region without another explanation
Tender pretibial nodules (erythema nodosum) appearing alongside respiratory symptoms in a young adult from the Ohio or Mississippi valley area
emergencySigns: Oxygen saturation below 92% at rest or rapidly progressive dyspnea with bilateral lung infiltrates following a high-inoculum exposure — acute respiratory distress syndrome-equivalent requiring urgent antifungal and possibly mechanical ventilation
Fever, pancytopenia, hepatosplenomegaly, and elevated LDH in an HIV-positive patient with CD4 below 150 cells/µL — disseminated histoplasmosis requiring urgent amphotericin B
Altered mental status, seizures, or meningeal signs in a patient with known or suspected histoplasmosis — CNS dissemination with potential for fatal meningitis

Superior vena cava syndrome or severe dysphagia from mediastinal mass effect in known histoplasmosis — fibrosing mediastinitis or compressive lymphadenopathy requiring urgent imaging and surgical or interventional assessment

causes.cause: Inhalation of Histoplasma capsulatum microconidia from disturbed soil
causes.description: H. capsulatum grows in mold form in moist, nitrogen-rich soil. Disturbance by wind, construction, excavation, or human activity aerosolizes microconidia (2-5 µm) that deposit in the alveoli. Conversion to pathogenic yeast form at 37°C initiates infection. The fungus is endemic in the Ohio, Mississippi, Missouri, and St. Lawrence river valleys.

causes.cause: Occupational or recreational exposure to bird or bat habitats
causes.description: Bird roosting sites (starling roosts, chicken coops, pigeon lofts) and bat caves concentrate H. capsulatum in the soil. Spelunkers, farmers, demolition workers, and landscapers disturbing such environments face the highest exposure doses. A single brief cave entry can transmit thousands of spores.

causes.cause: Demolition and renovation of old buildings in endemic areas
causes.description: Historic buildings harboring bird colonies or soil-contaminated materials release large spore clouds during demolition. Point-source outbreaks affecting dozens to hundreds of workers or bystanders have been documented in Indianapolis, Cincinnati, and Kansas City, with incubation periods clustering at 10-14 days.

Remote subclinical infection may reactivate when cell-mediated immunity declines. Corticosteroids, TNF-α inhibitors (used for rheumatoid arthritis and IBD), AIDS (CD4 <150 cells/µL), and organ transplantation are the most common triggers. Reactivation dissemination carries a mortality of 15-30% without treatment.

Travel to endemic regions — the US Midwest, Central and South America, sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia — followed by illness after return can present diagnostic challenges. Clinicians unfamiliar with histoplasmosis may not consider fungal etiology, delaying diagnosis by weeks to months.

Even normal immunity can be overwhelmed when spore burden is extremely high. Mass exposures at demolition sites have caused acute illness in 50-80% of exposed workers simultaneously. The probability of symptomatic disease scales directly with the estimated inhaled spore dose.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to the Ohio/Mississippi river valley
riskFactors.description: Endemic transmission occurs most intensely in a belt stretching from Texas to Ohio. Long-term residents have often had subclinical exposure; newcomers lack immunity. Returning travelers from endemic regions of Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia account for a growing share of cases in non-endemic US cities.

HIV is the most powerful risk factor for progressive disseminated histoplasmosis; at CD4 counts below 150 cells/µL the risk of dissemination from any exposure increases dramatically. AIDS-defining histoplasmosis is reportable in most US states.

Patients receiving infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept, or high-dose corticosteroids have markedly impaired granuloma formation, enabling fungal replication. The FDA issued black-box warnings on TNF inhibitors for endemic fungal infections in 2008.

riskFactors.factor: Solid-organ or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation
riskFactors.description: Calcineurin inhibitors, mycophenolate, and post-transplant immunosuppression suppress the T-cell response needed to contain histoplasma. Histoplasmosis occurs in roughly 0.5-2% of solid-organ transplant recipients in high-endemic areas.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational soil disturbance in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Demolition workers, construction crews, farmers, landscapers, and pest-control workers face repeated high-dose exposures. Protective respirators (N-95 or higher) reduce inhaled spore burden substantially.

Pre-existing cavitary or bullous lung disease creates dead-space environments where H. capsulatum can establish chronic infection, analogous to pulmonary tuberculosis. Chronic cavitary histoplasmosis requiring long-term antifungal therapy is almost exclusively a disease of emphysematous lungs.

Infants under 2 years lack mature cellular immunity and are at disproportionate risk of rapid dissemination from primary infection. Adults over 65 years, particularly those with comorbidities, have higher rates of progressive disease and worse outcomes.

affectedDemographics: United States: highest seroprevalence in Ohio (up to 90% in adults), Indiana, Tennessee, Kentucky, and Missouri. An estimated 500,000 new infections annually (CDC).
Latin America: endemic throughout Mexico, Central America, and northern South America; histoplasmosis is a leading AIDS-defining illness in Brazil, Colombia, and Venezuela.

Sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia: growing recognition of H. capsulatum var. duboisii (African histoplasmosis) and var. capsulatum in immunocompromised populations, particularly in HIV-endemic regions.
Occupational groups: demolition workers, miners, cave guides, and farmers in endemic regions face exposures 3-5× the background population rate.
Immigrant and traveler populations in non-endemic cities: tropical medicine clinics in New York, London, and Amsterdam see increasing imported cases among HIV-positive migrants from Latin America.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis of acute pulmonary histoplasmosis requires integrating exposure history, clinical presentation, and laboratory findings. The diagnostic pathway begins with a thorough occupational and travel history — the single most powerful clue is a flu-like respiratory illness appearing 1-3 weeks after a documented high-risk exposure (demolition, spelunking, bird cleaning) in an endemic area. Chest radiograph typically shows patchy infiltrates, hilar or mediastinal adenopathy, or a miliary pattern; findings can be subtle or absent in mild disease. CT of the chest is more sensitive and may reveal nodular opacities that calcify over months, a radiographic signature of resolved histoplasmosis.

Antigen detection in urine and serum by enzyme immunoassay (EIA) is the fastest test available, with sensitivity of 83-92% for acute pulmonary disease and over 95% for progressive disseminated histoplasmosis; cross-reaction with blastomycosis occurs in 10-15% of cases. Serology (complement fixation and immunodiffusion) becomes positive 4-8 weeks after infection — useful for diagnosis of subacute or chronic disease but may be negative in the first 2-3 weeks and in severely immunosuppressed patients. Culture of respiratory specimens, bronchoalveolar lavage, blood, or bone marrow remains the gold standard and identifies the fungus in 70-90% of disseminated cases but requires 2-4 weeks. For mild acute disease in immunocompetent patients with characteristic exposure history and chest imaging, empiric antifungal treatment is reasonable without waiting for culture confirmation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects polysaccharide antigen in urine — fastest and most sensitive single test for moderate-to-severe and disseminated disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard urine sample. Results in 1-3 days from most reference labs. Sensitivity 83-92% for acute pulmonary disease; higher in immunocompromised patients. Antigen level falls with successful treatment and can monitor response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antigen in blood; often combined with urine antigen for maximum sensitivity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Useful when urine antigen is negative but clinical suspicion is high. Particularly valuable in disseminated disease where fungemia is present.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects antibody response; positive 4-8 weeks after infection; useful for chronic and subacute disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results typically in 3-7 days. M and H precipitin bands on immunodiffusion are highly specific. May be negative in early acute disease or in immunosuppressed patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes pulmonary infiltrates, hilar/mediastinal adenopathy, nodules, and calcifications — guides severity assessment and differential diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute scan lying on a table. No radiation equivalent to a nuclear medicine test but does involve low-dose X-ray exposure. Reports back within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Fungal culture (sputum, BAL, blood, or bone marrow)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive identification of H. capsulatum; required for species confirmation, antifungal susceptibility, and atypical presentations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum or BAL obtained during bronchoscopy under light sedation. Blood cultures drawn from peripheral vein. Growth appears in 2-4 weeks on selective fungal media. Positive in 70-90% of disseminated cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage and biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Obtains respiratory samples for culture, cytology, and silver staining; essential when non-invasive tests are negative and diagnosis uncertain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient or day-case procedure under moderate sedation. A flexible scope passes via nose or mouth into airways. Throat soreness and mild cough for 24 hours afterward. Results from microbiology within 1-4 weeks.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment decisions in histoplasmosis depend on severity, immune status, and clinical form. Mild acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in immunocompetent patients resolves spontaneously, and antifungal therapy is not required unless symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks. The Infectious Diseases Society of America (IDSA) 2007 and 2020 updated guidelines recommend oral itraconazole 200 mg three times daily for 3 days then 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 weeks as first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe acute disease. Itraconazole achieves serum levels adequate for inhibiting H. capsulatum yeast and is well-tolerated; the capsule formulation requires an acidic gastric environment and should be taken with a full meal or a cola beverage, while the solution has better bioavailability but causes more GI side-effects.

For severe acute disease with diffuse pulmonary infiltrates or respiratory failure — particularly following massive inoculum exposure — intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day is given for 1-2 weeks, followed by de-escalation to oral itraconazole to complete 12 weeks total. Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis in AIDS or other immunocompromised patients requires liposomal amphotericin B induction for 2 weeks followed by itraconazole for at least 12 months, with secondary prophylaxis (itraconazole 200 mg daily) maintained until immune reconstitution is achieved (CD4 >150 cells/µL on antiretroviral therapy for at least 6 months). Drug-drug interactions between itraconazole and immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) require close therapeutic drug monitoring. Serum itraconazole concentrations should be measured after 2 weeks of therapy to confirm adequate exposure (target trough >1 µg/mL). Urine antigen monitoring at 1-month intervals tracks treatment response; a rising antigen level after initial decline suggests treatment failure or reinfection.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (200 mg three times daily × 3 days loading, then 200 mg twice daily × 6-12 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antifungal for moderate-to-severe acute pulmonary and disseminated histoplasmosis. Taken with food or cola beverage for optimal absorption. Capsule formulation preferred over solution for tolerability. Drug interactions common — check before prescribing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates 80-90% in IDSA-guideline eligible patients; symptom resolution within 2-4 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B (3-5 mg/kg/day IV, 1-2 weeks induction)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred for severe acute disease, respiratory failure, CNS histoplasmosis, and disseminated disease in immunocompromised patients. Lipid formulation reduces nephrotoxicity compared to conventional amphotericin B deoxycholate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate over 85% in severe disseminated histoplasmosis when initiated promptly; mortality reduced from >25% to <5% in AIDS patients with adequate therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Methylprednisolone (0.5-1 mg/kg/day IV, 1-2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunct for severe hypoxia, acute respiratory distress syndrome, and histoplasmosis-associated pericarditis or mediastinal inflammation. Not recommended routinely; used alongside antifungal therapy to limit inflammatory injury.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data suggest improved oxygenation and shorter ICU stay when added to antifungals in acute respiratory distress syndrome-like presentations

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole or posaconazole (salvage therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line azoles for patients who fail or cannot tolerate itraconazole. Voriconazole 200 mg twice daily or posaconazole extended-release 300 mg daily are options supported by case series. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited data; estimated response rate 60-75% based on case series and registry data for refractory disease

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole secondary prophylaxis (200 mg daily, ongoing)
medicalTreatments.description: Maintained in HIV-positive patients with CD4 <150 cells/µL after completing initial treatment to prevent relapse. Discontinued once CD4 exceeds 150 cells/µL on antiretroviral therapy for at least 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Relapse rate reduced from 30-50% to under 5% with ongoing prophylaxis in AIDS patients on antiretroviral therapy

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical decompression or stenting of mediastinal structures
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for mediastinal granuloma or fibrosing mediastinitis causing superior vena cava syndrome, bronchial obstruction, or esophageal compression. Antifungal therapy does not improve fibrosing mediastinitis; mechanical relief is the only option.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Variable — symptom palliation in 50-70% but recurrence common in fibrosing mediastinitis; outcomes better in granulomatous than fibrosing disease

alternativeTreatments.name: Fluconazole (alternative in itraconazole-intolerant patients)
alternativeTreatments.description: Fluconazole 400-800 mg daily has activity against H. capsulatum but is significantly less effective than itraconazole. IDSA guidelines designate it as an alternative when itraconazole is unavailable or not tolerated, based on lower in vitro potency and inferior clinical outcomes in comparative studies.

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist should evaluate any suspected moderate-to-severe acute histoplasmosis, all immunocompromised patients with positive antigen testing, and anyone with mediastinal complications. Diagnosis can be challenging in non-endemic areas where histoplasmosis is not immediately considered; specialist guidance prevents unnecessary antibiotics and delays in antifungal initiation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist with experience in endemic mycoses. Major academic medical centers in the Midwest, South, and those with tropical medicine programs see the highest case volumes. For HIV-associated disease, co-management with HIV medicine specialists optimizes antiretroviral and antifungal drug interactions.
hospitalCriteria: Inpatient admission for hypoxia (O2 saturation <92%), respiratory failure, or bilateral infiltrates on CT
HIV-positive patient with positive antigen testing regardless of initial severity
Suspected disseminated histoplasmosis with pancytopenia, hepatosplenomegaly, or CNS signs
Facilities with bronchoscopy, fungal culture capabilities, and access to IV liposomal amphotericin B
Antigen testing available on-site or via rapid reference laboratory
keyFacilities: Infectious disease specialty clinics at academic medical centers in endemic US states
HIV/AIDS treatment centers with experience in endemic mycoses
Tropical medicine and travel medicine outpatient clinics
Tertiary pulmonology services with bronchoscopy capability
preventionStrategies: Wear NIOSH-approved N-95 or higher respirators when disturbing soil, demolishing old buildings, or entering bat caves in endemic regions.
Wet down soil or debris with water before excavation to reduce aerosolization of spores during high-risk activities.
Avoid high-risk environments (bird roosts, bat-inhabited caves, old chicken coops) if immunocompromised; consult an infectious disease physician before traveling to highly endemic areas.
Consider itraconazole prophylaxis (200 mg daily) before anticipated high-risk occupational or environmental exposure in severely immunocompromised patients, as per IDSA guidance.
Screen HIV-positive patients with CD4 below 150 cells/µL and exposure history with urine Histoplasma antigen, even without symptoms, to enable pre-emptive treatment.
lifestyleModifications: Take itraconazole capsules consistently with a full meal to maximize absorption; avoid antacids, H2-blockers, and proton pump inhibitors which reduce capsule bioavailability.
Abstain from alcohol during itraconazole therapy to minimize drug-related hepatotoxicity; liver function tests should be monitored monthly during prolonged treatment.
Rest and avoid strenuous aerobic activity during the acute symptomatic phase — myalgia and fatigue typically last 1-3 weeks.
Maintain adequate caloric and protein intake to support immune recovery during convalescence.
Attend follow-up antigen testing at 1 and 3 months to confirm treatment response and detect early relapse.
Disclose histoplasmosis history to all future treating physicians, particularly before any planned immunosuppressive therapy.

recommended: Protein-rich foods (eggs, lean meat, legumes) to support immune function and tissue repair
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation if on itraconazole long-term, which can rarely affect bone metabolism
Cola beverages with itraconazole capsule doses to optimize gastric acid absorption
Well-balanced diet with adequate micronutrients to support recovery
avoid: Alcohol during antifungal therapy, which increases hepatotoxicity risk
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice, which inhibit CYP3A4 and may increase itraconazole levels unpredictably
Antacids, PPIs, and H2-blockers taken simultaneously with itraconazole capsules
Undercooked or raw foods that introduce additional infection risk in immunocompromised patients

exerciseGuidelines: During acute symptomatic illness, rest is recommended as fatigue and myalgia are prominent. Once fever resolves and symptoms improve (usually 2-4 weeks), gradual return to light aerobic activity is appropriate. Patients with moderate or severe disease should wait until chest imaging shows improvement before resuming exercise. Immunocompromised patients with disseminated disease should follow infectious disease specialist guidance before returning to full activity.
dailyManagement: Take itraconazole at consistent times with meals; use the capsule formulation and avoid acid-suppressing medications.
Monitor for side-effects of itraconazole: ankle swelling, hypertension, headache, or jaundice — report immediately.
Track fever, cough, and fatigue daily and note any new symptoms such as joint pain, rash, or neurological changes.
Keep all follow-up appointments for serum antigen monitoring at 1 and 3 months after starting therapy.
In HIV-positive patients, maintain antiretroviral therapy adherence to achieve immune reconstitution, which is critical for long-term histoplasmosis control.
Avoid further high-risk exposures (demolition, spelunking, bird roost cleaning) until fully recovered and antifungals completed.
prognosis: Prognosis for acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in immunocompetent individuals is excellent: over 95% of cases resolve spontaneously or with a brief course of itraconazole. Fever typically subsides within 1-2 weeks and fatigue within 4-6 weeks. Residual pulmonary nodules and hilar calcifications persist on imaging but cause no symptoms. Patients with moderate disease treated promptly with itraconazole recover fully in 90-95% of cases without lasting lung impairment. Prognosis worsens substantially in immunocompromised patients: progressive disseminated histoplasmosis in AIDS patients not on effective antiretroviral therapy historically carries a 1-year mortality of 25-50%; with liposomal amphotericin B induction, itraconazole maintenance, and ART adherence, mortality falls below 10% in recent cohorts. Fibrosing mediastinitis is the one form that does not respond to antifungal therapy and carries a long-term morbidity burden from progressive mediastinal obstruction.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and constitutional symptoms resolve in 1-2 weeks with antifungal treatment. Cough and dyspnea improve over 2-4 weeks. Urine Histoplasma antigen normalizes within 3-6 months of effective therapy. Full energy and exercise tolerance return within 4-8 weeks in immunocompetent patients. Immunocompromised patients may require 12 months or longer of antifungal therapy with more gradual functional recovery.
complications: Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis: hematogenous spread to liver, spleen, adrenals, CNS, and bone marrow in immunocompromised patients — detect early with antigen testing, CBC, and liver function tests.

Fibrosing mediastinitis: irreversible fibrosis of mediastinal structures causing superior vena cava syndrome, bronchial stenosis, or esophageal obstruction — not responsive to antifungals; requires structural interventions.
Pericarditis: inflammatory pericardial effusion complicating mediastinal lymphadenitis — monitor for tamponade with echocardiography if dyspnea or hemodynamic change occurs.
Pulmonary hypertension: rare but described late complication of fibrosing mediastinitis with progressive pulmonary artery stenosis.
Adrenal insufficiency: bilateral adrenal destruction by H. capsulatum in disseminated disease — screen with morning cortisol in any patient with unexplained hypotension or electrolyte abnormalities.
Relapse in immunocompromised patients: HIV-positive patients stopping secondary prophylaxis prematurely have 30-50% relapse rates — monitor CD4 and antigen levels carefully.

supportResources.name: IDSA Histoplasmosis Practice Guidelines
supportResources.description: Official clinical practice guidelines from the Infectious Diseases Society of America, updated recommendations for diagnosis and treatment.

supportResources.name: CDC Histoplasmosis Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient-facing information on exposure risk, symptoms, testing, and prevention from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: Mycoses Study Group Education and Research Consortium (MSGERC)
supportResources.description: Research consortium focusing on endemic fungal infections with patient education resources and clinical trial information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both are dimorphic fungal pneumonias causing flu-like illness with hilar adenopathy, but Valley fever is geographically restricted to the arid US Southwest, Arizona/California, and northern Mexico. Coccidioidomycosis antigen testing and serology are distinct from histoplasma tests; eosinophilia is more prominent in Valley fever. Itraconazole treats both, but disseminated coccidioidomycosis often requires fluconazole.

confusedWithConditions.name: Blastomycosis (acute pulmonary)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Blastomycosis also inhabits the Ohio and Mississippi valleys and presents with fever and pulmonary infiltrates, but skin lesions (verrucous or ulcerated) and bone involvement are hallmarks of blastomycosis not typical of histoplasmosis. Histoplasma antigen cross-reacts with blastomycosis in 10-15% of cases; culture remains definitive.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce granulomatous lung disease with hilar adenopathy, calcifications, and constitutional symptoms. TB is diagnosed by acid-fast sputum smear, culture, and PCR, whereas histoplasmosis requires antigen testing or fungal culture. Empiric TB treatment without antifungal is the most common mismanagement error when histoplasmosis is not considered.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial community-acquired pneumonia responds to antibiotics within 48-72 hours; histoplasmosis does not. Hilar and mediastinal adenopathy on CT strongly favors histoplasmosis over typical bacteria. Antigen testing discriminates with high sensitivity.

relatedConditions.name: Blastomycosis (acute pulmonary)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are dimorphic fungal pneumonias endemic to the Ohio and Mississippi valleys, often managed identically with itraconazole.

relatedConditions.name: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley fever)
relatedConditions.relevance: Sister endemic mycosis occupying arid Southwestern geography; both cause self-limited flu-like respiratory illness with granuloma formation and occasionally severe dissemination.

relatedConditions.name: Aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another pulmonary fungal infection requiring antifungal therapy; both enter via inhalation and cause significant disease in immunocompromised patients.

relatedConditions.name: Acute miliary tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares miliary radiographic pattern, mediastinal adenopathy, and constitutional symptoms with severe histoplasmosis; an important diagnostic differential requiring separate workup.

faqs.question: What is histoplasmosis and how do you get it?
faqs.answer: Histoplasmosis is a fungal lung infection caused by Histoplasma capsulatum, acquired by inhaling microscopic spores from soil contaminated with bird or bat droppings. Activities like demolishing old buildings, spelunking in bat caves, or disturbing soil near bird roosts in the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys carry the highest risk.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: Acute histoplasmosis causes fever, chills, dry cough, fatigue, headache, and muscle aches appearing 3-17 days after exposure. Hilar lymph node swelling is a key imaging finding. Joint pain with shin nodules (erythema nodosum) occurs in about 5% of cases. Severe disease causes shortness of breath and requires antifungal treatment.

faqs.question: Does histoplasmosis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most immunocompetent people. Mild acute pulmonary histoplasmosis resolves spontaneously within 2-4 weeks. Antifungal treatment is needed only when symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks, disease is moderate or severe, or the patient is immunocompromised. Immunosuppressed patients almost always require treatment.

faqs.question: What antibiotic or antifungal treats histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: Histoplasmosis is a fungal infection and does not respond to antibiotics. Oral itraconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6-12 weeks is first-line treatment for moderate or severe disease. Severe cases or immunocompromised patients receive intravenous liposomal amphotericin B for 1-2 weeks followed by itraconazole.

faqs.question: How long does histoplasmosis last?
faqs.answer: Mild acute histoplasmosis lasts 2-4 weeks. Moderate disease treated with itraconazole requires 6-12 weeks of therapy with gradual symptom improvement over the first 2-4 weeks. Disseminated histoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients may need 12 months or more of antifungal treatment plus ongoing secondary prophylaxis.

faqs.question: Is histoplasmosis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Histoplasmosis does not spread from person to person. Infection requires direct inhalation of spores from contaminated soil or bat and bird droppings. There is no human-to-human respiratory or contact transmission documented in the medical literature.

faqs.question: Where is histoplasmosis most common in the US?
faqs.answer: Histoplasmosis is most common in the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys, including Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Missouri, Kentucky, Tennessee, and Arkansas. Up to 90% of adults who have lived long-term in some Ohio counties test positive for past exposure. The fungus also occurs in Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia.

faqs.question: How is histoplasmosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Urine Histoplasma antigen testing is the fastest method, with 83-92% sensitivity for acute disease. Serology (complement fixation) becomes positive 4-8 weeks after infection. Chest CT often shows infiltrates and hilar adenopathy. Fungal culture of sputum or bronchoscopy samples confirms diagnosis but takes 2-4 weeks to grow.

faqs.question: Can histoplasmosis be fatal?
faqs.answer: In immunocompetent people, histoplasmosis is rarely fatal. In HIV-positive patients with CD4 counts below 150 cells per microliter, untreated disseminated histoplasmosis historically carries mortality above 25%. With liposomal amphotericin B induction and itraconazole maintenance, mortality falls below 10% in treated patients.

faqs.question: Can histoplasmosis spread to other organs?
faqs.answer: Yes, in immunocompromised patients. Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis spreads to the liver, spleen, adrenal glands, bone marrow, mouth, and central nervous system. Adrenal destruction causing adrenal insufficiency and CNS histoplasmosis with meningitis are the most serious disseminated complications.

faqs.question: Is histoplasmosis the same as Valley fever?
faqs.answer: No. Histoplasmosis and Valley fever (coccidioidomycosis) are both dimorphic fungal lung infections but are caused by different fungi. Histoplasmosis is caused by Histoplasma capsulatum and is endemic to the Midwest; Valley fever is caused by Coccidioides immitis and is endemic to the arid US Southwest and California.

faqs.question: What does histoplasmosis look like on chest X-ray?
faqs.answer: Chest X-ray in acute histoplasmosis typically shows patchy infiltrates in one or both lungs, hilar or mediastinal lymph node enlargement, or a miliary (snowstorm) pattern in heavy exposure. Old calcified granulomas and calcified hilar lymph nodes are permanent residual signs of prior resolved infection.

faqs.question: Can children get histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, children are susceptible, and infants under 2 years are at particularly high risk of rapid dissemination because their cellular immunity is immature. Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in otherwise healthy older children follows the same mild course as adults. Itraconazole is used for treatment in pediatric patients with dosing adjusted by weight.

faqs.question: Does histoplasmosis cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: Most cases of acute pulmonary histoplasmosis heal without permanent lung damage. Residual calcified nodules and calcified lymph nodes persist but do not cause symptoms or impair lung function. Chronic cavitary histoplasmosis in emphysematous lungs can cause progressive lung destruction if untreated.

faqs.question: How do I know if my histoplasmosis treatment is working?
faqs.answer: Fever and constitutional symptoms should begin improving within 2 weeks of starting itraconazole. Urine Histoplasma antigen levels, measured at 1 and 3 months, serve as objective markers of treatment response. A falling antigen level confirms efficacy; a rising level after initial decline suggests relapse or reinfection.

symptomKeywords: shin nodules joint pain respiratory infection
metaDescription: Histoplasmosis: fungal lung infection from bird and bat droppings, endemic to the US Midwest. Symptoms, itraconazole treatment and prognosis reviewed.
